rulings.Case Title: JOSEPH CONNELLY VS. COLLEEN HOLLAND et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery Requests And Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, Line 1, Plaintiff'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Discovery Requests And Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff moves to compel responses to special interrogatories, form interrogatories and requests for production. The motion was initially calendared for hearing on October 17, 2013. It was then taken off calendar pending bankruptcy hearings. By order dated April 7, 2014, the Court granted plaintiff's ex parte application to re-calendar the original motions, and set them for hearing on April 25, 2014. At the same time, the Court ordered courtesy copies delivered to the Court with exhibits separated by exhibit tabs. The parties did not update the pleadings or provide tabbed exhibits in advance of the April 25 hearing. During the pendency of the original briefing, defendant provided supplemental responses, narrowing the issues in dispute. The Court now grants in part and denies in part the motions, as follows. There are two primary remaining disputed issues. The first is whether defendants' communications with her brother, a lawyer, are privileged during the time prior to the brother's formal representation of defendant in this matter. The Court finds those communications are privileged and denies the motions to the extent they seek those communications. The second issue is whether information about a company called VegiWeb is subject to discovery. The Court finds that the discovery aimed at VegiWeb is excessive, and generally not reasonable calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, and therefore denies the motions to the extent they seek discovery about VegiWeb. The exception to this ruling relates to a small number of discovery requests that seek information about cross-use of employees or funds between VegiNews and VegiWeb. Accordingly, the motions are denied except as to Special Interrogatories 91, 98, 102-103, 127-128, 131-132, and 139-140. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFF PETER BOWES Motion To Compel Party Defendants' Depositions Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion is granted. Plaintiff moves to compel attendance at deposition by the defendants. Plaintiff timely filed notices of deposition, although some confusion arose as to whether they sought documents (plaintiff's counsel clarified on the record that they did not). Since then, defendants' counsel has moved to withdraw. That motion is set for hearing on May 2. The Court grants the motion to compel attendance at deposition, subject to providing substitute counsel, if one is permitted to appear, a reasonable time to schedule the deposition after May 2. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW V. SMITH VS. THE OPERA PLAZA RESIDENTIAL PARCEL HOMEOWNERS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, Line 7: CROSS DEFENDANT ANDREW SMITH'S DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT. Off calendar. Cross-Defendant filed a notice of motion and a memorandum of points and authorities but did not file or serve the demurrer. (Cal. Rule of Court 3.1112; see also Weil & Brown, Cal. Prac. Guide: Civ. Proc. Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2013) par. 7:98, "The demurring party must file with the court, and serve on the other party, the following: Demurrer; Notice of Hearing; Memorandum of Points and Authorities; Proof of service.") Additionally, the notice and memorandum of points and authority do not specify the statutory grounds for the demurrer. (CCP sec. 430.60.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: SHANA COOPER et al VS. LUXOR CAB COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Amended Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Mental Examination Of Pltf Michael Sims
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, Line 3, DEFENDANTs LEONARD ALBERT, LUXOR CABS, INC.'s (ERRONEOUSLY SUED AND SERVED) Amended Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Mental Examination Of Plaintiff Michael Sims Pro Tem Judge Geoff Howard, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is denied. Defendant has not demonstrated that plaintiff seeks recovery for mental and emotional distress over and above that usually associated with the physical injuries claimed. Further, the meet and confer between the parties supports the view that plaintiff does not seek recovery for mental and emotional distress over and above that usually associated with the physical injuries claimed and does not intend to rely at trial, in support of a damages claim, on expert testimony regarding the usual mental and emotional distress associated with plaintiff's physical injuries. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to geoff.howard@bingham.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MANUEL MENDOZA, AN INDIVIDUAL VS. JOSEPH TUMANG, AN INDIVIDUAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, line 16. PLAINTIFF MANUEL MENDOZA'S Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatories. The motion to compel is granted in part and denied in part. Granted as to Form Interrogatories 2.13 and 15.1, 16.7 and 20.8. The motion to compel as to Form Interrogatories 16.2 and 16.3 denied. The motion to compel is granted as to Form Interrogatory 17.1. Defendant must separately answer the form interrogatory with a particularized response for each request for admission that it denies. The omnibus answer provided is not comprehensible. Additional responses shall be served within fifteen days of the notice of entry of this Order. Both parties' requests for sanctions are denied. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. rulings.Case Title: RONALD N WILSON VS. WESTSIDE COMMUNITY SERVICES et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, April 25, 2014, line 19. DEFENDANT WESTSIDE COMMUNITY MENTAL HEALTH CENTER'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained, with 10 days leave to amend, as to the third cause of action, and overruled as to the first, second, and fourth causes of action. For the first cause of action for breach of written contract, a written contract may be pled either in haec verba or according to legal effect. (Twaite v. Allstate Ins. Co. (1989) 216 Cal.App.3d 239, 252.) In this case, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts to allege the legal effect of the written fee agreement; Plaintiff alleges that on November 23, 2010, the parties entered into a written agreement where Plaintiff would perform legal services at a rate of $ 350.00/hour. (Complaint, 3.) For the second cause of action for quantum meruit, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts. Plaintiff may plead inconsistent facts. (Mendoza v. Rast Produce Co., Inc. (2006) 140 Cal.App.4th 1395, 1402.) Defendant's reliance on Hedging Concepts, Inc. v. First Alliance Mortgage Co. (1996) 41 Cal.App.4th 1410 is inapposite at the pleading stage because there has been no finding of fact that would preclude recovery based on an implied contract. For the third cause of action for account stated, Plaintiff has not pled sufficient facts. Plaintiff has not alleged that Defendant agreed that the amount stated is correct or that Defendant promised to pay the stated amount. (CACI no. 373(2) & (3).) Plaintiff is granted leave to amend, if he can do so in good faith, to allege that Defendant agreed that the amount stated is correct and Defendant promised to pay the stated amount. For the fourth cause of action for open book account, Plaintiff has pled sufficient facts. (CACI no. 372.) An open book account may consist of a single entry reflecting the establishment of an account. (Joslin v. Gertz (1957) 155 Cal.App.2d 62, 66.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)
